content: 4.5 g of aluminium (Atomic mass 27 amu ) is deposited at cathode from $\mathrm{Al}^{3+}$ solution by a certain quantity of electric charge. The volume of hydrogen produced at STP from $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions in the solution by the same quantity of electric charge will be
explanation:
1 mole of Al, i.e. 27 g of Al are deposited by charge $=3 \mathrm{~F}$

$\therefore 4.5 \mathrm{~g}$ of Al will be deposited by charge

$$=\frac{3}{27} \times 4.5 \mathrm{~F}=0.5 \mathrm{~F}$$

2 F of charge liberate $\mathrm{H}_2=1 \mathrm{~mol}=22.4$ litre at STP

$\therefore 0.5 \mathrm{~F}$ of charge will liberate $\mathrm{H}_2=\frac{22.4}{2} \times 0.5$

= 5.6 L at STP

content: How long should water be electrolysed by passing through 100 amperes current so that oxygen released can completely burn 27.66 g of diborane? (Weight of $B=10.8 \mathrm{u}$ )
explanation:
To determine how long water should be electrolyzed with a current of 100 amperes to release sufficient oxygen to completely burn 27.66 g of diborane, consider the following:

The complete combustion reaction is:

$ \mathrm{B}_2 \mathrm{H}_6 + 3 \mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{~B}_2 \mathrm{O}_3 + 3 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} $

Given that 27.66 g of $\mathrm{B}_2 \mathrm{H}_6$ corresponds to 1 mole of $\mathrm{B}_2 \mathrm{H}_6$, it requires 3 moles of $\mathrm{O}_2$ for complete combustion. The electrolysis of water can be represented as:

$ \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_2 + \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2 $

For $\frac{1}{2}$ mole of $\mathrm{O}_2$, 2 Faradays of electricity are required. Therefore, to produce 3 moles of $\mathrm{O}_2$, the requirement is:

$ 3 \text{ moles of } \mathrm{O}_2 = 3 \times 2 \times \text{Faraday} = 12 \text{ Faraday} $

The total charge $Q$ (in coulombs) needed is calculated as:

$ 12 \times 96500 = i \times t $

Given that $i = 100 \, \text{A}$, substitute into the equation:

$ 12 \times 96500 = 100 \times t \quad \rightarrow \quad t = \frac{12 \times 96500}{100} = 3.2 \, \text{hours} $

Thus, the time required for electrolysis is 3.2 hours.

content: The ionic conductance of following cation in a given concentration are in the order.
explanation:
Conductance of anion in aqueous solution is dependent upon its hydrated size.

Ionic conductance $$\propto \frac{1}{\text { ionic size of hydrated ion }}$$

Thus, ionic conductance in aqueous solution increases in the order

$$\mathrm{Li}^{+}<\mathrm{Na}^{+}<\mathrm{K}^{+}<\mathrm{Rb}^{+}<\mathrm{Cs}^{+}$$. As the size of hydrated ion is minimum for $$\mathrm{Cs}^{+}$$ ion and maximum for Li ion among all the hydrated alkali metal ions.

content:
Consider the following cell reaction.

$$\begin{aligned} & 2 \mathrm{Fe}(s)+\mathrm{O}_2(g)+4 \mathrm{H}^{+}(a q) \longrightarrow \\ & 2 \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}(a q)+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(l), E^{\circ}=1.67 \mathrm{~V} \\ \end{aligned}$$

At $$[\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}]=10^{-5} \mathrm{M}, {\mathrm{P}}{ }_2=3 \mathrm{~atm}$$ and $$\mathrm{pH}=2.5$$, the cell potential at $$25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$$ is
explanation:
The half reaction are

$$\begin{aligned} & \left.\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{s}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}\right] \times 2 \text { [at Anode] } \\ & \frac{\mathrm{O}_2(g)+4 \mathrm{H}^{+}+4 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \text { [at cathode] }}{2 \mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{O}_2(g)+4 \mathrm{H}^{+} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(l)} \\ \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & E=E^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{4} \log \frac{\left(10^{-5}\right)^2}{\left(10^{-5}\right)^4 \times 3} \\ & =1.67+\frac{0.0591}{4} \log 3 \times 10^{-5} \times 10^{-5} \simeq 1.529 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned}$$

content: When an electrolytic solution conducts electricity, the current is carried by
explanation:
When an electrolytic solution conducts electricity, the current is indeed carried by the movement of ions within the solution. An electrolytic solution is made when an electrolyte dissolves in water or another solvent, dissociating into its constituent ions. These ions are charged particles: cations (positively charged) and anions (negatively charged).

Cations migrate towards the cathode (the negative electrode), and anions migrate towards the anode (the positive electrode) under the influence of an electric field in the solution. This movement of ions constitutes the electric current in the solution. Thus, Option B - cations and anions - is the correct answer. Electrons do not carry the current in the solution itself; instead, they move through the external circuit connecting the anode and cathode. Neutral molecules and the atoms of the electrolyte do not play a direct role in conducting electricity in the solution.

explanation:
Molecular formula is $$\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_3 \mathrm{NaO}_2$$. Electrolysis of aqueous solution occurs as on

Cathode $$\mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+e^{-} \longrightarrow \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_2(g)$$

Anode $$2 \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_3 \mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_6(g)+2 \mathrm{CO}_2(g)+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}$$
